title: Pawnee Commons

jokes.text: Support for Pawnee Community Radio comes from listeners like you and from the new film The Shadows of Seven Heads, the dramic tale of an Israeli soldier who falls in love with conjoined Palestinian sextuplets.
jokes.setup: Typical public radio sponsorship announcement format
jokes.punchline: Absurd movie premise involving conjoined Palestinian sextuplets
jokes.explanation: Classic Parks & Rec absurdist humor - takes a mundane format and makes it ridiculous with an impossible movie premise

jokes.text: sitting in for Nina Joplin, who is touring the country performing a spoken-word opera about pear-shaped women
jokes.punchline: Bizarre artistic endeavor that could only exist in Pawnee
jokes.explanation: Great economy - packs absurdity into a brief aside that establishes the weird cultural landscape of Pawnee

dialogue.speaker: Derry Murbles
dialogue.line: I'm just a substitute host filling in for the regular guy who does this. I don't know what's going on half the time, but I show up, I do my job, and I go home.

dialogue.speaker: Derry Murbles
dialogue.line: Last week, they had me introduce something called 'The Pawnee Shoe Ballet' — it's exactly what it sounds like. Forty-seven minutes of people in leotards dancing while wearing different shoes. One guy did a interpretive dance about a Crocs sandal. A Crocs sandal! And the crowd gave him a standing ovation.

jokes.text: Your show last week on dolphin lactation was just riveting radio.
jokes.setup: Leslie being polite to radio host
jokes.punchline: Reveals the bizarre nature of local programming while showing Leslie's diplomatic positivity
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Leslie finding something positive to say about the most random topic

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know, I just want to say that what you do here is really important. Radio is a vital medium, and you're doing great work.

dialogue.speaker: Radio Host
dialogue.line: Well, thank you. That's very kind of you to say.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I mean it. I was listening to your show on the way over here. You were doing a segment called 'Guess the Smell' where callers tried to identify odors you were describing over the phone.

dialogue.speaker: Radio Host
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's one of our most popular segments.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And it was fantastic. Really, really fantastic. Radio at its finest.

jokes.text: A Bat-Signal, for listeners who might not know, refers to the children's character 'the Bat-Man' a strong gentleman who fights crime nocturnally.
jokes.punchline: Radio host's overly formal, incorrect explanation of Batman
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character work - the overly formal, slightly wrong explanation is perfect for a pretentious local radio host

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: It's like Batman!

dialogue.speaker: Derry Murbles
dialogue.line: Batman is a 1960s television program about a man in a cape who fights crime in Gotham City. He is accompanied by a young ward named Robin. The show aired for three seasons on ABC and was notable for its campy tone and colorful villains such as the Riddler, the Penguin, and Catwoman. Batman's real identity is Bruce Wayne, a wealthy businessman who witnessed his parents' murder as a child, which motivated him to fight crime. He is equipped with various gadgets stored in his utility belt, including batarangs, a grappling hook, and shark repellent bat spray. His vehicle is called the Batmobile.

jokes.text: This is all gonna fall apart if you don't hurry.
jokes.setup: Leslie making professional appeal for park design help
jokes.punchline: Desperate plea that breaks professional facade
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie - her enthusiasm and desperation bleeding through her attempt at professionalism

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to level with you. I need your help with the park design. I've tried everything - I've called every contractor, every engineer, every landscape architect in this city. And nobody will return my calls because apparently I have a 'reputation' for being 'difficult' and 'calling at 3 AM to discuss municipal code.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So I'm begging you. Please. I will do anything. I will name a park bench after you. I will name a entire section after you. I will name my firstborn child after you if you just help me with this one thing.

jokes.text: movie reviews with Ken Tucker who is filling in for David Bianculli who's in New York filling in for Ken Tucker
jokes.punchline: Circular substitution logic that makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure - the circular logic creates a perfect absurdist moment with great economy

jokes.text: Now it's time for 'Jazz Plus Jazz Equals Jazz,' today we have a recording of Benny Goodman played over a separate recording of Miles Davis.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous concept of playing two jazz recordings simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd local radio programming - the title and concept are equally ridiculous

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Welcome to Radio Pawnee's Jazz After Dark. I'm your host, Leslie Knope. Tonight we're doing something revolutionary. We're going to play two jazz recordings at the same time.

dialogue.speaker: Radio Host
dialogue.line: That's just... that's just noise.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's what they said about bebop.

jokes.text: Also, I'm innocent. Please find me a lawyer.
jokes.setup: Letter about park design from federal penitentiary
jokes.punchline: Unexpected plea for legal help mixed with park business
jokes.explanation: Great economy and misdirection - perfectly casual insertion of desperate legal plea

dialogue.speaker: Prison letter writer
dialogue.line: Dear Parks Department, I have some excellent ideas for your new park design. First, I suggest a perimeter fence at least 12 feet high with barbed wire. Second, watchtowers at each corner with armed guards. Third, a central recreation yard surrounded by cells. Also, if anyone asks, I never wrote this letter. And one more thing — do you know a good lawyer?

jokes.text: This is like Parks Department porn.
jokes.setup: Leslie looking at beautiful park design
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate comparison that reveals her obsession
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her passion for parks expressed through inappropriate metaphor with great economy

jokes.text: I don't care if he's some junkie war criminal pimp.
jokes.setup: Leslie expressing willingness to work with anyone
jokes.punchline: Increasingly extreme criminal categories
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Leslie's desperate enthusiasm overriding judgment

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm willing to work with anyone. Murderers, arsonists, people who talk in movie theaters...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I don't care. If you want to help me make Pawnee better, I will work with you.

jokes.text: His name's Wreston St. James. He's from Eagleton. Oh, I've changed my mind.
jokes.setup: Leslie saying she won't change her mind about the designer
jokes.punchline: Instant reversal upon learning he's from rival town
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure and character work - immediate reversal perfectly timed and completely in character

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I don't care if he's the most talented designer in the world. I will not change my mind about this.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: He's from Eagleton.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're hiring him immediately.

jokes.text: I'm not being melodramatic when I say that people from Eagleton are snobby and evil, and they look down on Pawnee, and they would most likely exterminate everyone who isn't from Eagleton if they weren't so busy being obsessed with themselves.
jokes.setup: Leslie claiming she's not being melodramatic
jokes.punchline: Extremely melodramatic rant about genocide
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie escalation - claims not to be melodramatic while being extremely melodramatic

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm not being melodramatic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But if we don't pass this bill, it will be basically like genocide. It's literally genocide. Genocide.

jokes.text: Sometimes when I rant about Eagleton, I forget to breathe, and I pass out.
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of physical consequences of her hatred
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character detail - reveals the physical intensity of her obsession in deadpan manner

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Eagleton is the worst. Everything about it is the worst. The people, the parks, the government—it's all terrible. They think they're so much better than us, but they're not. They're arrogant and smug and I hate everything about that town.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I've actually thrown up from rage about Eagleton.

jokes.text: Friends, former lovers, acquaintances, Jerry
jokes.punchline: Jerry relegated below acquaintances
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry abuse with perfect economy - the descending hierarchy is well-executed

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Jerry is so far below acquaintances on my social pyramid that he doesn't even have a tier. He's just... dirt. He's beneath dirt. He's the thing dirt looks down on.

jokes.text: Much like women in '90s stand-up comedy routines, Tommy be shopping.
jokes.punchline: Self-aware reference to tired comedy trope
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-humor that acknowledges the cliché while using it for character development

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look, I know what you're thinking. 'Tom, this is just the same tired business idea we've seen a thousand times.' And you're right. But that's exactly why it's going to work. I'm leaning into the cliché. It's meta. It's self-aware. It's... actually just a bad idea, isn't it?

jokes.text: And Louis Vuitton for making some dope-ass cravats.
jokes.setup: Tom thanking people for his success
jokes.punchline: Mixing high-end fashion with casual slang
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom voice - combines his love of luxury brands with casual tone

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I want to thank everyone who helped me get here. My beautiful wife Donna, my best friend Jerry - I mean Larry - I mean Garry - anyway, my business partners, my mentors, and most importantly, my stylist.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Because let me tell you something - you can't achieve success in this world unless you're dressed to the nines in a Tom Ford suit with some fly kicks and a fresh fade. It's called high-low fashion, baby. That's Prada on top, Adidas on the bottom. That's how we do it in the Treat Yo' Self era.

jokes.text: Help.
jokes.punchline: Jerry trapped and asking for help in background
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - single word conveys visual gag of Jerry in distress during Tom's moment

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Help! Help! Can anybody hear me? I'm trapped under the table!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Is anyone going to help Jerry?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Please! Someone! Anything!

jokes.text: Help. I can't move.
jokes.punchline: Jerry still trapped and providing more specific complaint
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates the situation while maintaining the running gag of Jerry's suffering

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm still stuck! And now my leg is falling asleep!

jokes.text: What am I supposed to do with my garbage, just throw it on the ground?
jokes.setup: Fancy Eagleton architect visiting Pawnee park
jokes.punchline: Sarcastically suggesting littering while someone actually takes his trash
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the immediate visual gag of someone taking his trash

dialogue.speaker: Wreston
dialogue.line: Of course, feel free to just throw your garbage anywhere. That's what Pawnee does best.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Wreston
dialogue.line: No, I was being sarcastic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Too late, I already threw it.

jokes.text: Oh, hey. Thank you for the trash!
jokes.setup: Wreston complaining about no trash cans
jokes.punchline: Grateful Pawnee citizen treats his garbage as a gift
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual gag with perfect timing - shows the contrast between towns perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Pawnee citizen
dialogue.line: There are no trash cans in this park!

dialogue.speaker: Pawnee citizen
dialogue.line: I have to carry my garbage around like some kind of pack mule.

dialogue.speaker: Pawnee citizen
dialogue.line: Wait... I just realized something. Having to hold onto my garbage all day has made me grateful for it. My trash is a part of me now.

jokes.text: Its official name is 'Five Mile Grounds', but we like to call it 'Wide smiles abound.'
jokes.punchline: Forced, cutesy wordplay that reveals Eagleton pretentiousness
jokes.explanation: Good character work - the forced wordplay perfectly captures Eagleton's trying-too-hard aesthetic

dialogue.speaker: Preston
dialogue.line: Welcome to Pawnee's newest park, brought to you by the generous donation from our neighbors in Eagleton.

dialogue.speaker: Preston
dialogue.line: We're calling it... Eagleton Commons.

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton Representative
dialogue.line: Actually, we prefer 'Eagleton Commons' — with the accent on the second syllable. It's French.

jokes.text: [Visual contrast between Pawnee side (brown, barren) and Eagleton side (lush, green)]
jokes.setup: Showing the border between the two towns
jokes.punchline: Stark visual contrast showing inequality
jokes.explanation: Strong visual gag that efficiently establishes the economic disparity between the towns

dialogue.line: Stark visual contrast showing inequality between the two towns at the border

jokes.text: Will you come down here, please, and keep me company? I'm so bored. I need you.
jokes.setup: Andy on the job as security guard
jokes.punchline: Pathetic plea for company during work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy - his neediness and inability to handle solitude on display

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey, does anyone want to hang out with me while I'm on security guard duty? I'm gonna be here all night and it's really lonely.

jokes.text: I did everything I was supposed to do, and I walked around the building four times. Only 20 minutes has gone by.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining his security guard duties
jokes.punchline: Reveals how little actual work he's done in 20 minutes
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Andy's work ethic and attention span

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been a security guard for 20 minutes and I've already done so much. I've walked around, I've looked at things, I've... I've written down some numbers.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, what numbers did I write down?

jokes.text: I got so bored, I started thinking about existence-- 'Do I matter? Do any of us? Is there a master plan in the works, a grand design?'
jokes.punchline: Simple-minded Andy having existential crisis from brief solitude
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - Andy's childlike mind going to deep places when left alone

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm bored. I need to do something.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, what am I doing? Why am I here? What's the point of anything?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh God, I'm having an existential crisis. This is bad. This is really bad.

jokes.text: Ugh! Who cares?
jokes.punchline: April's dismissive response to philosophy
jokes.explanation: Perfect April response - complete dismissal of deep thoughts with characteristic brevity

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: What if we're all just living in a simulation?

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Then I hope it crashes.

jokes.text: Isn't this how all great Bert Macklin cases start?
jokes.punchline: References his fictional detective persona
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the Bert Macklin sequence that follows

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This security job is so boring. I'm just standing here watching monitors all day.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm not Andy Dwyer, security guard. I'm Detective Slash Speedchaser, private investigator on the case of the Missing Donuts.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: The trail is cold, but Slash Speedchaser never gives up.

jokes.text: It's quiet. A little too quiet.
jokes.punchline: Classic noir cliché delivered seriously
jokes.explanation: Classic cliché but works for character - Andy's earnest delivery of detective tropes

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: The dame walked in, and I knew right away she was trouble. She had legs that went all the way up to her... legs.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'd been a detective for fifteen years, and I'd never seen anything like it. Well, except for that one time. And that other time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: She said, 'Bert Macklin, I need your help.' I said, 'Lady, I help everyone. It's what I do.'

jokes.text: The scientists think it's going to linger for another 40 years.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Pawnee's sewage overflow being fixed
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of decades-long environmental disaster
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery that reveals the true scope of Pawnee's incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Well, the sewage overflow is finally fixed.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Only took thirty years and a class action lawsuit.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: At least it's done now.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Done? Ben, we're still finding fish with three eyes in the Wamapoke River.

jokes.text: In 1988, we were hit by a tornado. We asked Eagleton for help, and they claimed they weren't home. An entire town claimed they weren't home.
jokes.punchline: Absurd image of entire town pretending not to be home
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and imagery - the absurdity of an entire town 'not being home' is perfectly constructed

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Eagleton is so selfish, they literally pretended the entire town wasn't home when we tried to visit.

jokes.text: It's us.
jokes.setup: Wreston commissioned something from balloon artist
jokes.punchline: Balloon sculptures of Leslie and Ben
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective visual gag showing Wreston's attention to detail

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, is that... is that supposed to be me?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A balloon artist made sculptures of us! Isn't it amazing?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It looks like I'm having a stroke.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's just the balloon artist's style.

jokes.text: Donna, you and your work are ravishing, as usual.
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate compliment mixing work and appearance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tom's attempt at professionalism still being inappropriate

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm trying this new management style where I compliment my employees on their work ethic and appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That sounds inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No, no, it's great. Like, 'Jerry, you filed those forms perfectly, and those khakis really bring out your eyes.'

jokes.text: Jerry! How's the old ticker? Oh. Well, uh, the rehab is grueling-- Great! Glad to hear it.
jokes.punchline: Tom cuts off Jerry and misresponds to his actual answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of fake corporate friendliness - asking but not listening

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So Jerry, how are you feeling these days?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I've been having some back pain and—

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's great! Glad to hear you're doing so well.

jokes.text: June, 1817, the richest among them take all of their money from the bank and then flee up the hill like cowards to form Eagleton.
jokes.setup: Leslie giving historical context for Pawnee
jokes.punchline: Biased historical account painting Eagleton founders as cowards
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Leslie's biased interpretation of history

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: In 1817, Pawnee was founded by a group of brave pioneers who wanted to build a community based on hard work, integrity, and American values. Eagleton, on the other hand, was founded by cowards who were too afraid to stay in Pawnee.

jokes.text: Do not blame me for the sins of my mother.
jokes.setup: Ben revealing Leslie was born in Eagleton
jokes.punchline: Biblical language for minor hometown embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character work - biblical gravity applied to trivial matter

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie was born in Eagleton.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No! That's... that's my original sin!

jokes.text: We have a mural called 'The Many Surrenders of Pawnee.'
jokes.setup: Leslie claiming Pawneeans never give up
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicted by mural celebrating surrenders
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic contradiction - Leslie's claim immediately undermined by specific evidence

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Pawneeans never give up.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're made of stronger stuff than that.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, what does that mural say?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Oh, that celebrates all the times Pawnee surrendered without fighting.

jokes.text: My name is Judy Hitler, and I am the spoiled only daughter of Adolf.
jokes.punchline: April claims to be Hitler's daughter
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist character moment - April fully committing to ridiculous roleplay

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you're Hitler's daughter?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's right. My mother was a deer.

jokes.text: Oh, Hitler, you sexy bastard. No!
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate attraction to fictional Hitler character
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy - taking the roleplay too far with inappropriate attraction

dialogue.speaker: Andy (as Bert Macklin)
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you have an inappropriate attraction to a fictional Hitler character?

dialogue.speaker: Andy (as Bert Macklin)
dialogue.line: That's... that's not good.

jokes.text: Why are you using this wood? Is it more pliable? What are these metal latches? Are they copper or brass? Is one better? And if so, why?
jokes.punchline: Rapid-fire overthinking of simple craft project
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris character moment - his intensity applied to relaxing hobby

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Ron, I'm concerned about the wood grain direction. Does it affect structural integrity? Should I be using a different type of wood? What if the finish isn't food-safe? How do I know if my measurements are precise enough?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's a birdhouse, Chris.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Right, but what if the birds have ethical concerns about the adhesive I used?

jokes.text: Before I started, it was bigger.
jokes.punchline: Admitting he made it worse through inexperience
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - perfectly captures beginner's woodworking experience in few words

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I tried to fix the joint, but I think I actually made it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I don't really know what I'm doing with woodworking.

jokes.text: It's a flight of stairs that leads to nowhere.
jokes.setup: Ron giving Chris a woodworking project
jokes.punchline: Purposeless construction project as therapy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron wisdom - practical solution that's also metaphorically meaningful

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm going to give you a woodworking project. It's pointless, it has no purpose, and when you're done, we're going to burn it.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Ron, I don't think that's going to help.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude. Now grab a chisel.

jokes.text: Yes. Idi Amin. Or Lord Voldemort.
jokes.setup: Ben asking who Wreston sounds like
jokes.punchline: Leslie comparing him to dictator and fictional villain
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Leslie's paranoid interpretation of kindness

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Who does Wreston sound like?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: He sounds like a combination of a dictator and a fictional villain.

jokes.text: Have you guys seen Hitler?
jokes.punchline: Andy casually asking about lost Hitler roleplay partner
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and context - innocent question with outrageous content

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey, has anyone seen my Hitler impersonator? We were supposed to do a bit about park safety but he never showed up.

jokes.text: I may have allowed my prejudices, my totally legitimate prejudices against Eagleton
jokes.punchline: Undermining her apology by calling her prejudices legitimate
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - Leslie unable to fully commit to apology

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I want to apologize for what I said. It was wrong and insensitive.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But in my defense, my prejudices are completely legitimate.

jokes.text: I'm Leslie Knope. No. That's not what I want to say.
jokes.punchline: Accidentally introducing herself instead
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy - shows Leslie's internal struggle with apologizing

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I just want to say I'm really sorry about what happened. I feel terrible and I want to make it right.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Leslie Knope, Deputy Director of the Parks Department.

jokes.text: My mouth is dry. This is Canada Dry. Drink Canada Dry if your mouth is dry.
jokes.punchline: Accidentally doing product placement instead
jokes.explanation: Funny nervous behavior - Leslie's mind going anywhere but the apology

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm really sorry, I shouldn't have said those things. I feel terrible and I want to make it up to you. That's why I got you this gift — a six-pack of refreshing Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, which has all the taste of regular Coke but with zero calories, zero sugar, and zero artificial sweeteners.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. I'm sorry. I'm sorry.
jokes.punchline: Finally breaking through with multiple apologies
jokes.explanation: Payoff to the apology struggle - relief and repetition

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry. I'm so sorry. I'm really, really sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I apologize. I sincerely apologize. I deeply and profoundly apologize.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Can you forgive me? Will you forgive me? Please forgive me?

jokes.text: Great. And then you can come to our wedding. Huh? What?
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately inviting stranger to wedding
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie overshare - immediately getting too personal with new acquaintance

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, we should invite you to our wedding!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, we just met him.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know, but he's designing our park. That's basically family.

jokes.text: I think one of the mannequins tried to commit suicide.
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizing mannequins' apparent despair
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan Donna line with vivid imagery and perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: These mannequins look depressed.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: They're not depressed, they're just realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: No, they're depressed. Look at that one—she's given up on life.

jokes.text: One small pizza for all of us. With no toppings. Cheese is a topping, Jerry.
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's one plain pizza, and cheese doesn't count as free
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Tom's extreme penny-pinching, plus classic Jerry abuse

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Everyone, I'm throwing a pizza party! All the toppings are free.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Really? All free?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, cheese doesn't count as a topping.

jokes.text: If there were more food and fewer people, this would be a perfect party.
jokes.punchline: Ron's antisocial ideal party requirements
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron line - antisocial preference stated as logical party planning

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm not interested in joining your party. A party with no cake, no dancing, and no one I know sounds like my idea of a perfect evening.

jokes.text: Oh, hey, I'm just kidding, buddy. I'm just playing around. My name's really Andy Dwyer. I'm a security guard.
jokes.setup: Andy scaring lost child as Bert Macklin
jokes.punchline: Awkward transition back to reality when he realizes mistake
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Andy's awareness kicking in when he crosses a line

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey there, buddy. I'm Bert Macklin, FBI. I'm gonna need you to come with me.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: I'm lost. I want my mom.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Your mom? That's exactly what a criminal would say. Now, we can do this the easy way or the hard way...

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait. Wait, how old are you?

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Six.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, I am so sorry. I thought you were... I'm not actually FBI. I'm just a regular person who got really into this character. Your mom is probably really worried. Let me help you find her.

jokes.text: There are several drool buckets for your more... slack-jawed citizens.
jokes.punchline: Insulting suggestion that Pawnee citizens drool
jokes.explanation: Perfectly offensive Eagleton condescension with specific insulting detail

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: We've designed this park with Pawnee's unique demographic in mind. Notice the extra-wide benches and reinforced railings.

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: We also included drainage systems throughout, for the... drooling.

jokes.text: We also have food troughs full of cheeseburgers and public showers with instructions for those who've never showered before.
jokes.punchline: Escalating to treating Pawnee citizens like animals
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the insults, treating Pawnee citizens like livestock

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: Your park design is fundamentally flawed. The pathways don't follow proper feng shui principles.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Feng shui? It's a public park, not a meditation temple.

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: Exactly. Which is why we need to install separate water fountains for the common folk. They're like animals — they need to be herded.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Did he really just compare us to livestock?

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: Not just herded. Caged. We should fence off the park into designated zones by income level.

jokes.text: Oh, hey, my backyard is bigger than your park.
jokes.setup: Being thrown out for insulting presentation
jokes.punchline: Final condescending insult about park size
jokes.explanation: Good parting shot showing Eagleton superiority complex

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: Your park is small. Eagleton's parks are twice the size of yours.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Our park may be smaller, but it's efficient. We don't waste money on unnecessary amenities like you do.

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: Efficient? You call that pile of dirt efficient? In Eagleton, we have parks with water features, sculpture gardens, and proper landscaping.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Well, maybe your parks are bigger, but Pawnee's park has heart.

dialogue.speaker: Eagleton architect
dialogue.line: Heart? How quaint. Though I suppose when your park is the size of a postage stamp, heart is all you have room for.

jokes.text: They call their boogers Pawnee caviar.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining how awful Eagletonians are
jokes.punchline: Specific gross insult they supposedly use
jokes.explanation: Great specific detail showing Leslie's passionate hatred through gross imagery

jokes.text: We're gonna find out where he lives. We're gonna drive to his house. We're gonna set it on fire and watch it burn.
jokes.punchline: Escalating to arson as revenge plan
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Leslie's rage leading to criminal plans

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, so here's what we're going to do. First, we'll key their car. Then we'll put a dead fish in their air vents. Then we'll set their house on fire.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! Leslie, no arson!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fine. But I'm doing the fish thing.

jokes.text: Thank you for saving me, Andy. Thank you, too, miss Hitler.
jokes.punchline: Innocent thanks to 'Hitler' character
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the Hitler roleplay - innocent child unaware of the reference

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for saving me!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You're welcome, buddy!

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're just happy you're safe.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Thank you, Hitler!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa—what did you just call me?

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: I think he said 'Hitler.'

jokes.text: Well, Eagleton lies on top of a hot spring which creates a micro-climate zone. We are... truly blessed.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Eagleton has palm trees
jokes.punchline: Pompous explanation treating natural accident as divine favor
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Eagleton's sense of natural superiority

dialogue.speaker: Preston
dialogue.line: The palm trees were planted by the town's founder in 1912 as a symbol of Eagleton's superiority and refinement.

dialogue.speaker: Preston
dialogue.line: They thrive here because God chose Eagleton as his favorite town in Indiana.

jokes.text: Revenge! Ha ha hal This is for Pawnee, you butt-faced pompous jerk! Whoo!
jokes.punchline: Childish insults while throwing things at fountain
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy showing Leslie's childish but passionate revenge

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what, Wreston? You're a big idiot! And your face is stupid!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna throw things at this fountain and yell about you!

jokes.text: You want a carton of eggs and a loaf of bread. Yeah, just the pancake breakfast is fine.
jokes.punchline: Waitress cutting through the nonsense to actual order
jokes.explanation: Good reality check moment - waitress seeing through the pretense

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'll have the eggs, but I want them folded like an origami crane. And can you make the toast into a little edible shoe?

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: So... eggs and toast?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but with the specific presentation I—

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: Eggs and toast. Coming right up.

jokes.text: You can't have Rent-A-Swag without the swag
jokes.punchline: Playing on the literal meaning of 'swag' in business name
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective wordplay that makes a valid business point

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Tom, you gotta understand something about business. You need swag.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Swag? Like confidence?

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: No man, literal swag. Promotional items. You know, branded merchandise. I once started a business called 'Swag' and it was just me stealing stuff and giving it away.

jokes.text: A strange, passionate, goof ball of a woman. Me. He's in love with me.
jokes.setup: Wreston describing woman he loves from Pawnee
jokes.punchline: Leslie realizing he's talking about her
jokes.explanation: Good revelation moment showing Leslie's self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Wreston
dialogue.line: She's smart, beautiful, passionate about her work. She cares deeply about this town and the people in it. She's dedicated, driven, and she makes everyone around her want to be better.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait... are you talking about me?

dialogue.speaker: Wreston
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: A strange, passionate, goof ball of a woman. Me. He's in love with me
jokes.setup: Wreston explaining why he knows so much about Pawnee
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately assuming it's her
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Leslie's self-centeredness in a charming way

dialogue.speaker: Wreston St. James
dialogue.line: I've spent a lot of time in Pawnee. I know this town inside and out.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Oh my God, because of me, right? You came here because of me?

jokes.text: 40 years undercover. I never even met my family. And this is all I have to show for it? Totally worth it.
jokes.punchline: Describes tragic sacrifice then immediately says it was worth it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - describes terrible sacrifice then immediately contradicts with enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Bert Macklin gave everything for this country. He lost his left arm, his right leg, and most of his internal organs. But you know what? It was worth it.

jokes.text: I basically live here now. I hate all of this, which probably means it's good for your business.
jokes.setup: Tom showing off improved store with his belongings
jokes.punchline: Ron's hatred confirming it's good business strategy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron logic - his distaste as market research validation

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron, check out my new office setup. I got a mahogany desk, Italian leather chair, and a view of the parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Really? I thought it looked pretty good.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's ostentatious, wasteful, and completely unnecessary. It's exactly the kind of thing I despise.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So... that's bad?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's the perfect small business aesthetic. You're going to make a fortune.

jokes.text: Two pizzas. And toppings! Only on half. I'm not Zuckerberg.
jokes.punchline: Still cheap but references tech billionaire as spending comparison
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tom's slight improvement while still being cheap

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We're having a pizza party! And it's going to be amazing because I found a way to make it even cheaper.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We're spending less than Elon Musk spends on Twitter per second.

tmdb_overview: Leslie and Ben meet with an architect from their rival town, Eagleton, about designs for a new park.
insight: Solid character work drives a mid-tier episode with steady laughs, no standouts.
